headline: Indiana Professor Walks Earth's Circumference in 14 Years
meta_description: Russell Mumper completed a 24,901-mile walking goal over 14 years while working full-time, taking 55 million steps and wearing out 49 pairs of shoes.
summary_short: An Indiana University researcher achieved his audacious goal: walking Earth's circumference in 14 years while maintaining a full-time career.
body:
Russell Mumper crossed the finish line on his driveway on June 25—not with fanfare, but with his wife holding a checkered flag and two family dogs at his side. The Indiana University Vice President for Research had just completed a 24,901-mile walk begun in 2012, a goal that consumed fourteen years of his life.

What makes this achievement remarkable isn't just the distance. Mumper logged 5,900 separate walks while working full-time and moving his family across state lines three times. He averaged about 5 miles per day, wore out 49 pairs of trail shoes, and took approximately 55 million steps. The elevation gain and loss he accumulated equals climbing and descending Mount Everest 46.5 times.

He reached this milestone at 60 years old, driven not by a desire for fame but by genuine love for walking and hiking. His daughter Shannon reflected on his character: "My dad is the most hardworking man I know."

Rumper approached his journey with scientific precision, tracking every statistic along the way. As he reflected afterward, "I had no idea how long it would take." Yet that uncertainty didn't deter him. His commitment demonstrates that extraordinary goals aren't always about dramatic moments—they're built from thousands of quiet, consistent steps taken one day at a time.
